Astrophysics Expo · Pair-Instability Supernovae

The stars that
vanish completely

Most massive stars leave behind a neutron star or black hole when they die. But a rare class of ancient stellar giants explodes so completely that nothing remains; that is the PAIR INSTABILITY SUPERNOVAE when a population lll star dies.

Population III stars

Pair-instability supernovae only happen to Population III stars, the very first stars to form after the Big Bang. They are formed from pristine primordial gas: hydrogen, helium, and trace lithium only. No heavier elements existed yet.

Mass range
60–300 M☉
Surface temperature
~100,000 K
Lifespan
~2–3 Myr

ColourExtremely blue-white

LifespanA few million years
Cross-section of a massive star. Radiation pressure and gravity are in balance. Pair production disrupts this equilibrium.
Stellar evolution tracks by metallicity. Pop III stars (zero metallicity) follow a distinct path uniquely vulnerable to pair instability.
Why they could grow so massive

Without heavy elements, Pop III stars could not shed mass through stellar winds as efficiently as modern stars, allowing them to grow to over 100 times the Sun's mass. The bigger the star, the hotter its core, and the more likely it is to trigger pair instability.

The key trigger is the helium core mass: roughly 35 M☉ (corresponding to about 85 M☉ on the main sequence). A candidate host galaxy for Pop III stars, CR7, has been observed at very high redshift (z ≥ 10).

No heavy elements Formed from Big Bang gas Top-left of HR diagram Burns out in millions of years He core > 35 M☉ triggers PISN

How pair instability works

Step through the collapse mechanism, from a stable giant to total annihilation in about 50 seconds.

Ordinary supernova (top) vs pair-instability supernova (bottom). In a PISN, photons form electron-positron pairs before iron ignition, triggering a runaway that completely destroys the star. Credit: Scientific American.
Core temperature vs density. The blue zone marks where pair instability strikes: a giant star enters it and explodes, while an ordinary star tracks to higher densities and iron-core collapse.
Inside the star: photons push outward (pressure) while gravity pulls inward. Pair production removes photons from the radiation field, tipping the balance irreversibly toward collapse.
The adiabatic index (Γ)

Think of Γ as measuring how hard a gas pushes back when compressed. A star stays alive as long as its gas can resist gravity. When pair production removes photons from the radiation field, Γ falls and the star loses that fight.

Value of ΓWhat it means
Γ > 4/3Stable: radiation pressure resists gravity
Γ = 4/3Marginally stable: a delicate balance
Γ < 4/3Unstable: gravitational collapse is inevitable

The explosion

The thermonuclear runaway completely destroys the star in about 50 seconds, leaving no neutron star, no black hole, and nothing but an expanding cloud of heavy elements and light.

Artist's impression of the final moments. The star is completely disrupted within 50 seconds and no compact remnant survives.
Runaway duration
~50 sec
Kinetic energy
10⁵² ergs
Ejecta speed
~10,000 km/s
Nickel produced
up to 50 M☉
3D CASTRO simulation of the PISN explosion. Many fluid instabilities occur during the blast, visible as turbulent filaments across the expanding core.
Density map (g/cm³) at t = 28 s. The hot ignition core (red) is surrounded by rapidly expanding, turbulent ejecta.
Ion temperature map (keV) at t = 28 s. The asymmetric structure reveals the chaotic, multi-core nature of the ignition.

All three frames: PISN with 100 M☉, multicore ignition. Simulated with the CASTRO hydrodynamics code.

Products of the explosion
ProductTypical yieldRole
⁵⁶Ni (radioactive nickel)Up to ~50 M☉Powers the months-long light curve via radioactive decay
Ejecta mass150–250 M☉Heavy elements scattered into surrounding space
Kinetic energy10⁵²–10⁵³ ergs10–100× more than a typical core-collapse supernova
Why the light curve looks different
PropertyCause
Extremely bright (10⁴⁴ erg/s)Enormous ⁵⁶Ni mass, roughly 50× a normal supernova
Slow rise: 50 to 100 daysLight must diffuse through massive, rapidly expanding ejecta
Lasts months to a year⁵⁶Co decay (77-day half-life) sustains the brightness
Smooth, gradual declineFollows the ⁵⁶Co decay rate, a clean radioactive tail

Decay chain: ⁵⁶Ni (6-day half-life) → ⁵⁶Co (77 days) → ⁵⁶Fe (stable iron). Each decay releases gamma rays that heat the ejecta and sustain the glow for months.

How astronomers identify a PISN

Pair-instability supernovae share their extreme brightness with other superluminous supernovae, but leave behind distinctive spectral and timing fingerprints.

Metallicity determines fate. PISN candidates need near-zero metallicity, found only in the earliest galaxies.
Remnants by mass regime. In the PISN zone (~130–250 M☉), the entire star is consumed and no compact remnant forms.
Comparison with other superluminous supernovae
FeaturePISNSLSN-I (magnetar)SLSN-II (H-rich)
SpectrumH-poor; O, Mg, Si, Ca linesH-poor; blue, featurelessHydrogen; narrow lines
Light curve rise50–100 days10–50 days50–100 days
Decline shapeSmooth; Ni/Co decayCan re-brightenOften double-peaked
Host galaxyLow metallicityDiverse hostsDiverse hosts
Late-time spectraStrong [O I], [Ca II], [Mg I]Weak or absentH and [O I] present
Key clue: the host galaxy

PISNe are expected only in very metal-poor environments, in primitive galaxies still resembling the early universe. The James Webb Space Telescope is now actively searching for these ancient explosions at the edge of the observable universe. A confirmed detection would be transformative for our understanding of the first stars.

Why this matters

Pair-instability supernovae shaped the chemical history of the universe and give us a rare window into the very first stars.

Webb's First Deep Field (SMACS 0723): thousands of galaxies stretching back to within a billion years of the Big Bang. JWST is now searching environments like this for pair-instability supernovae from the first stars.
Credit: NASA, ESA, CSA, STScI · Requires internet connection to display
The universe's most complete self-destructions

Unlike every other supernova type, a PISN completely unbinds the star. No neutron star, no black hole: every atom is expelled into space. The star is simply and entirely gone.

01
Chemical enrichment

PISNe scatter up to 250 M☉ of heavy elements into surrounding gas, seeding the early universe with the building blocks of later stars, rocky planets, and life.

02
A window to the first stars

We have never directly observed a Population III star. But PISNe are bright enough to detect across the observable universe. A confirmed detection would be transformative.

03
JWST and the cosmic dawn

JWST is searching the high-redshift universe for PISNe. A detection would probe conditions just a few hundred million years after the Big Bang.

Different endings for massive stars: a red supernova remnant, a neutron star, a planetary nebula, and at the top, the blinding light of a PISN. Unlike all the others, the PISN leaves no remnant behind.
Nothing is left beind after the supernovae.
3D CASTRO simulation: density at t = 28 s. The explosion's full power is already visible as ejecta expands rapidly outward.
An ordinary supernova leaves a neutron star or black hole. A pair-instability supernova leaves nothing at all. Credit: Scientific American.
